request.question: Which entertainment company is the creator of "The X Factor" the chief executive officer?

title: Bobby Mehta
Jonathan G. Ornstein
Fredric Tomczyk
Live Nation Entertainment
X Factor (Belgian TV series)
Glen Post
Simon Cowell
Charles Bunch
Mike Lunsford
B. Wayne Hughes
sentences: Siddharth N. "Bobby" Mehta was former CEO and vice chairman of HSBC North America.
Mehta served as an Advisor of TransUnion since December 31, 2012.
Mehta serves as consultant of TransUnion.
He served the chief executive officer and president of TransUnion from August 2007 to December 31, 2012, and Transunion Financing Corp. until December 31, 2012.
From May 2007 to July 2007, he served as a consultant to the board of directors at TransUnion.
He served as the chief executive officer and president of TransUnion until December 31, 2012.
He served as the chief executive officer of TransUnion LLC.
He served as chairman of the board and chief executive officer of HSBC Finance Corporation from April 2005 to February 2007.
He served as chief executive officer and president of TransUnion LLC from 2007 to 2012.
From 1998 to 2007, he held a variety of positions with HSBC Finance Corporation and HSBC North America Holdings, Inc.
Mehta served as chief executive officer of HSBC North America until February 2007.
Mehta served as consultant of TransUnion since May 2007 until July 2007.
Mehta served as group managing director of HSBC Holdings PLC of HSBC Finance Corp. since April 30, 2005, and its unit chief executive officer since March 2005.
He served as the chief executive of HS BC North America Holdings Inc., of HSBC Finance Corp., from March 2005 to February 15, 2007.
He served as an executive chairman of HSBC Financial Corporation Limited since April 2005 and served as its chief executive officer from April 2005 to February 15, 2007.
He served as the chief executive officer of HSBC Bank USA, N.A. until February 2007.
He served as the chief executive officer of HSBC North America Holdings Inc. since March 2005.
He served as chairman and chief executive officer of HSBC Financial Corp., Ltd.
He oversaw HSBC's global credit card services, its North American consumer lending and mortgage services businesses and its first mortgage operation.

He was also responsible for corporate marketing, strategic planning and corporate development for HSBC North America Holdings Inc. and had responsibility for the strategic management of credit cards throughout the HSBC Group.
Mehta served as group executive of Credit Card Services, Auto Finance and Canada of Household International Inc., since July 2002.
He worked at MasterCard’s U.S. region board since March 2000.
Mehta joined Household International Inc., in 1998.
He served as senior vice president of The Boston Consulting Group in Los Angeles and co-leader of Boston Consulting Group Financial Services Practice in the United States.
Mehta served as a director of Global Board of MasterCard Incorporated since March 17, 2005.
He served as unit chairman of HSBC Holdings PLC and served as its board member since March 2005.
He served as vice chairman and director of HSBC Financial Corporation Limited., (Formerly Household International Inc.).
He has been a director of Avant Credit Corporation since December 18, 2014.
He has been an independent director of The Allstate Corporation since February 19, 2014.
He serves as a member of the advisory board at Core2 Group, Inc.
He has been non-executive independent director at Piramal Enterprises Ltd since April 1, 2013.
He serves on the boards of Datacard, Chicago Public Education Fund, University of Chicago Laboratory Schools, The Economic Club of Chicago, The Field Museum and Myelin Repair Foundation.
He serves as a director of TransUnion Corp. and TransUnion LLC.
He served as a director of MasterCard International Inc. (also known as MasterCard Worldwide) (formerly, MasterCard Inc.), since March 17, 2005.
He served as a director of HSBC Financial Corp.
Ltd.
He has been a director of TransUnion since April 2012.
Mehta serves on the board of international advisors for the Monterey, California, Institute of International Studies and is a member of the Financial Services Roundtable.
He also serves on the board of advisors for the Myelin Repair Foundation.
Mehta holds a Bachelor of Arts in economics from the London School of Economics and Masters of Business Administration from the University of Chicago.
He stepped down as head of the North American unit after the lender raised its forecast for bad loans in the U.S.
He is of Indian descent.
Jonathan Ornstein is Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Mesa Air Group, Inc., and was appointed effective May 1, 1998.
From April 1996 to his joining the company as Chief Executive Officer, Ornstein served as President and Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of Virgin Express, a European airline.
From 1995 to April 1996, Ornstein served as Chief Executive Officer of Virgin Express Holdings, Inc.
Ornstein joined Continental Express as President and Chief Executive Officer in July 1994 and, in November 1994, was named Senior Vice President, Airport Services at Continental Airlines.
Ornstein was previously employed by the company from 1988 to 1994, as Executive Vice President and as President of the company’s WestAir Holding, Inc., subsidiary.
Fredric Tomczyk was the president and chief executive officer of TD Ameritrade from 2008 to 2016.
He served on the company’s Board of Directors from January 2006 until June 2007.

Before that he served as vice chair of corporate operations for TD Bank Group, executive vice president of retail distribution for TD Canada Trust, and president and chief executive officer of wealth management for TD Bank.
Before joining TD Bank, he was president and chief executive officer of London Life Insurance Company.
Live Nation Entertainment is an American global entertainment company, formed from the merger of Live Nation and Ticketmaster.
It owns, leases, operates, has booking rights for and/or equity interests in a large number of U.S. entertainment venues.

The leadership consists of Greg Maffei (chief executive officer of Liberty Media) as chairman and Canadian Michael Rapino (previously chief executive officer of Live Nation) as president and CEO of the company.
X Factor is the Flemish version of the British show "The X Factor" that was created by Simon Cowell.
The new "X Factor" series was a postcursor to "Idool 2003" / "2004" which features contestants & jury members from both seasons.
Glen F. Post III (born October 4, 1952) is the chief executive officer and president of CenturyLink, an S&P 500 integrated communications service provider based out of Monroe, Louisiana.
He earned a bachelor's degree in accounting in 1974 at Louisiana Tech University and an MBA in 1976 at Louisiana Tech.
Post joined CenturyTel in 1976.
He was named vice president in 1982 and was promoted to senior vice president and treasurer in 1984.
He was appointed to the CenturyTel board of directors in 1985, and the following year he was promoted to senior vice president and chief financial officer.
In 1988 Post was named executive vice president and chief operating officer.
He became the president and chief operating officer of CenturyTel in 1990.
In 1992 Post was named vice chairman of the board, president, and chief executive officer.
In 2002 he was appointed chairman of the board and chief executive officer.
Since 2009 Post has served as chief executive officer and president of CenturyLink.

His honors include: Louisiana Tech College of Administration and Business Distinguished Alumni in 1991, Louisiana Tech University Tower Medallion Award in 1997 and DeGree Enterprises Lifetime Achievement Award in Business 2003.
Simon Phillip Cowell ( ) (born 7 October 1959) is an English reality television judge and producer, entrepreneur, and philanthropist.

He is most recognised as a judge on the British TV talent competition series "Pop Idol", "The X Factor", and "Britain's Got Talent", and the American TV talent competition shows "American Idol", "The X Factor," and "America's Got Talent".
Cowell is the principal founder and chief executive of the British entertainment company Syco.
Charles E. Bunch (born c. 1950) is an American businessman who served as the chairman and chief executive officer of PPG Industries, Inc., until succeeded by Michael H. McGarry on September 1, 2015.

Since 2002, he had been a director and prior to becoming president and chief executive officer in March 2005 and chairman and chief executive officer in July 2005, Bunch was president and chief operating officer from July 2002.
He was the executive vice president from 2000 to 2002 and senior vice president of Strategic Planning and Corporate Services from 1997 to 2000.
Bunch is also a director of the H. J. Heinz Company and The PNC Financial Services Group, Inc.
He attended Georgetown University as an undergraduate and received his MBA from Harvard University in 1979.
Mike Lunsford is the chief executive officer of SK Planet, Inc., the U.S. arm of SK Planet, Ltd., a Korean-based company.

He is the former executive vice president and interim chief executive officer of RealNetworks, the former chief executive officer of Rhapsody, a joint venture between RealNetworks and Viacom, and the former president and interim chief executive officer of Earthlink.
Before joining EarthLink, Lunsford worked as a consultant at Andersen Consulting (now Accenture) in Chicago and Scott, Madden & Associates, a management consulting firm in Raleigh, North Carolina.
He received an undergraduate degree and a Masters of Business Administration (MBA) from the University of North Carolina.
Bradley Wayne Hughes (born September 28, 1933) is the founder and chairman of Public Storage, the largest self-storage company in America doing business as a REIT or real estate investment trust.
As of 2014, Hughes is worth $2.2 billion.

Known all his life by his middle name, B. Wayne Hughes was the company's President and Co-Chief Executive Officer from 1980 until November 1991 when he became Chairman of the Board and sole Chief Executive Officer.
He retired as Chief Executive Officer in November 2002 and remains Chairman of the Board.
He was Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer from 1990 until March 1998 of Public Storage Properties XI, Inc., which was renamed PS Business Parks, Inc. ("PSB"), an affiliated REIT.

From 1989-90 until the respective dates of merger, he was Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer of 18 affiliated REITs that were merged into the Company between September 1994 and May 1998 (collectively, the "Merged Public Storage REITs").
has been active in the real estate investment field for over 30 years.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

The `summary` must be a concise (typically 1–4 short sentences or clauses), self-contained intermediate state that helps the next agent locate the missing second-hop evidence. Do not try to produce the final answer. Follow these rules when composing the `summary`:

1) First-hop evidence: In one short clause, paraphrase or quote (<=15 words) the specific fact from the passages that is relevant to the question and that you are using as the bridge. Include the passage title or identifier that contains this evidence.

2) Bridge / missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity or the specific missing fact that must be resolved in the next hop (for example: an ambiguous given name that must be identified, the named person whose birthplace is required, the tournament title whose venues must be counted, or which film credit remains unverified). If the passages already give a full name, use that full name as the bridge entity; if only a given name or ambiguous identifier is present, state that the identity is unresolved.

3) Retrieval direction: Provide one concise, actionable next-hop retrieval plan (1–3 ordered steps or 1–2 short search-query templates). Use clear placeholders where appropriate (e.g., "fetch the biography page for <NAME>", "search \"<NAME> birthplace\" site:wikipedia.org", "open the '<TOURNAMENT>' page and inspect its Venues/Matches/Schedule section", or "fetch '<FILM>' cast list and compare to '<OTHER FILM>' cast"). Specify which page/title or which page sections the next agent should inspect. Do not perform retrieval or assert any facts that are not present in the given passages.

4) Disambiguation: If the question's referent is ambiguous (e.g., only a given name is provided), request the user's clarification in one short sentence only (e.g., "If you mean a different Maurice, please provide the full name."). Do not ask permission to search; just state the needed clarification.

5) Constraints and tone: Never invent or assert unverified facts (no guessed winners, years, birthplaces, counts, or cast overlaps). Do not output the final answer. Keep language neutral, concise, and actionable. Avoid long verbatim extracts beyond a short supporting quote.

Output exactly one field: `summary` (a single string following the above structure).
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `summary_1`, produce the field `query`.

Output requirements
- Return exactly one compact, single-line retrieval query string in the field `query`. Do not return any other fields, commentary, punctuation, quotes, or the answer.
- Keep the query short and retrieval-oriented (keyword bag, typically ~3–7 meaningful tokens). Prefer tokens already present in `question` or `summary_1`.

Rules for composing the query
1) Nonprofit medical-practice/facility questions: If the question asks about a non-profit/nonprofit medical practice or facility (or uses similar wording) and `summary_1` contains an organization name, do NOT repeat that organization name. Instead produce a concise bridge query combining the place token from the question, the state/region token, and a class term such as "nonprofit medical practice" or "clinic". Use the template: <place> <state> nonprofit medical practice clinic

2) Avoid hypothesizing names: If the needed facility or organization name is not supported by `question` or `summary_1`, do not invent or assert candidate names. Reuse the topic and the expressed information need (topic + class + location) to form the query.

3) Comparative person-role questions: If the question compares two people (e.g., which person fills a larger variety of roles) and the inputs name both persons or `summary_1` lists one person's roles, produce one compact query that names both people and includes concise role/occupation keywords (examples: director producer composer musician writer actor). You may include a brief source token such as Wikipedia to guide retrieval, but remain compact. Do not ask for the final comparison or return conclusions.

General prohibitions
- Do not output full sentences, long explanatory text, lists of candidate organizations, or claims/answers.
- Do not add metadata, instructions, or extra fields—only the single query string.

Produce the `query` field now according to the rules above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, and `passages`, produce the field `summary` (string). Preserve the native input/output interface exactly and return only the `summary` field. Do not produce any other fields, headers, or meta-commentary.

Task: Integrate the newly retrieved `passages` with the existing `context` so the result is a grounded, post-retrieval state that the final-answer agent can use. Keep the summary concise, factual, and limited to information present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`.

Required behavior (apply the relevant rules to each input):
- Preserve facts already asserted in `context` unless the `passages` contradict them. If passages contradict a contextual claim, state the contradiction briefly and retain uncertainty rather than inventing a resolution.
- When a passage (or passages) explicitly supports a claim found in `context`, do all of the following in the `summary`:
  1) Briefly restate the claim from `context` in one short clause.
  2) Identify which retrieved passage(s) provide explicit support by naming the passage title (or a short identifier) in parentheses and give a one-line paraphrase or at most one short quoted fragment drawn directly from that passage as corroboration. Keep paraphrases short and avoid long verbatim spans.
  3) If other passage(s) do not state the claim but supply useful metadata (for example: title, year, director, occupation, full name/alias, location details), list those retained metadata facts in one short clause per passage.
  4) Finish with one concise integration sentence that synthesizes the evidence: state which passage explicitly supports the claim and which passage(s) only supply metadata for downstream use. Resolve the earlier bridge (i.e., commit to the claim) only if explicit support is present; otherwise keep the summary phrased to reflect remaining uncertainty.
- If a passage links two entities (e.g., a facility name to an address or locality) and that link resolves a location or relationship asserted in `context`, include a single short paraphrase that records that linking fact and then a brief integrated sentence that ties that link back to the studio/person/film in question.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Output must be concise (about 1–4 sentences), factual, and limited to content present in the inputs. Prefer 1–3 sentences when the integration is simple.
- Do not add new facts, speculation, or external knowledge beyond `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
- Avoid long quotations; at most one short quoted fragment per supporting passage and only when it directly helps attribute support.
- Do not use hedging language when the passages state facts plainly. Use hedging only when the inputs are ambiguous or contradictory.
- Do not include dataset labels, internal notes, evaluation, or extraneous formatting. Do not produce the final answer outside this `summary` field.

Goal: deliver a compact, evidence-attributed summary that (a) restates the first-hop claim when present, (b) explicitly attributes which passage(s) support that claim and which provide metadata, and (c) synthesizes the evidence so the downstream final-answer agent can use it to answer the question.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional conservative, evidence-driven normalizations and tie-breaking rules (small, shared improvements):
- Use summary_2 as the later integrated evidence and give it priority when summaries disagree about the answer, but do not ignore an explicit short named-entity asserted clearly in summary_1: if either summary contains a concise explicit attribution (for example, phrasing like "directed by <NAME>", "headquartered in <PLACE>", or a direct statement that names the immediate jurisdiction), accept that explicit short named-entity as the supported minimal answer unless the other summary gives a different explicit short named-entity (in that conflicting-explicit case, emit a brief clarification question). In short: prefer explicit concise assertions wherever present; otherwise prefer summary_2 when resolving conflicts.
- When the two summaries agree exactly on a short named phrase that includes a trailing parenthetical disambiguator or trailing parenthetical abbreviation (final ' ( ... )'), strip that single final parenthetical and emit the pre-parenthesis core name (trim surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. Similarly, when the identical agreed phrase ends with the exact suffix ", United States", remove that trailing ", United States" and emit the remaining shorter place string.
- More generally, when an agreed short answer contains trailing clarifying material (a final parentheses block, a trailing parenthetical abbreviation, or an appositive beginning with a comma) and the core name up to the first '(' or the first comma appears verbatim in both summaries, emit that core substring rather than the longer qualified form.
- If the question explicitly requests a size (phrases like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size') and both summaries are identical and contain a phrase of the form '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less', emit only the '<size>' portion (number plus unit) verbatim as the minimal answer (remove the trailing inclusive qualifier).
- If the chosen minimal answer candidate is a contiguous digits-only token sequence (regex ^\d+$), convert it to its spelled-out English words (lowercase, with conventional hyphenation) and emit that word-form as the answer.
- For 'what do X and Y have in common' style questions where both summaries contain the same plural noun token as the shared attribute, emit the singular form of that noun (apply simple regular singularization rules such as removing a trailing 's' or converting 'ies' -> 'y').
- Always prefer a concise explicit short named-entity present verbatim in either summary over asking for clarification when the other summary merely reports inability or uncertainty; however, if the summaries provide two different explicit names for the same field, emit a concise clarification question rather than guessing.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Which entertainment company is the creator of "The X Factor" the chief executive officer?
hop1_docs: Live Nation Entertainment | Live Nation Entertainment is an American global entertainment company, formed from the merger of Live Nation and Ticketmaster.  It owns, leases, operates, has booking rights for and/or equity interests in a large number of U.S. entertainment venues.  The leadership consists of Greg Maffei (chief executive officer of Liberty Media) as chairman and Canadian Michael Rapino (previously chief executive officer of Live Nation) as president and CEO of the company.

Rod Roddenberry | Eugene Wesley "Rod" Roddenberry Jr. (born February 5, 1974) is an American television producer and the chief executive officer of Roddenberry Entertainment.  Eugene, the son of "Star Trek" creator Gene Roddenberry and Majel Barrett, is an executive producer on "", the first episode of which was released on September 24, 2017.

En Masse Entertainment | En Masse Entertainment, Inc. (formerly Bluehole Interactive, Inc.) is an American video game publisher and a subsidiary of South Korean video game developer Bluehole.  Founded in June 2008, the company is based in Seattle, Washington and is best-known for publishing massively multiplayer online role-playing game "TERA", which was developed by parent company Bluehole.  The original En Masse leadership team had extensive experience in the video game industry at leading companies, including Blizzard Entertainment, Microsoft, NCsoft, ArenaNet, and Electronic Arts.  In January 2013, Chris Lee and Soo Min Park became chief executive officer and chief operating officer, respectively.
Essgee Entertainment | Essgee Entertainment is a professional performing and publishing company formed in 1981 in Australia.  Its founder and chief executive officer is entertainer Simon Gallaher.

Hothead Games | Hothead Games Inc. is an independent Canadian video game developer based in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada.  The studio was founded in 2006 by Steve Bocska, Vlad Ceraldi and Joel DeYoung, all three of which were formerly employed by Radical Entertainment.  However, Bocska left the company again in 2007.  On 10 March 2009, Ian Wilkinson, who had been president and chief executive officer of Radical Entertainment since its foundation in 1991 until 2008, became president and chief executive officer of Hothead Games, replacing Ceraldi.  In turn, Ceraldi became the studio's director of game development, while DeYoung moved from his chief operating officer position to director of game technology.

Todd Miller (media executive) | Todd Miller is an American-born media executive and the Chief Executive Officer of Celestial Tiger Entertainment (CTE), a diversified media company headquartered in Hong Kong.  The company creates and distributes branded television services and is a venture between Saban Capital Group; Celestial Pictures, a company owned by Astro Overseas Limited; and Lionsgate, the world's largest independent filmed entertainment studio.

VOC Opperhoofden in Japan | "Opperhoofd" is a Dutch word (plural "opperhoofden") which literally means 'supreme head[man]'.  In its historical usage, the word is a gubernatorial title, comparable to the English chief factor, for the chief executive officer of a Dutch "factory" in the sense of trading post, as led by a factor, i.e. agent.  The Japanese called the Dutch chief factors "kapitan" (from Portuguese "capitão").
response.summary_1: "'Todd Miller is Chief Executive Officer of Celestial Tiger Entertainment' (passage [6]); Bridge: the missing fact is which entertainment company the creator of 'The X Factor' (the creator's name/identity is not given in the passages) is chief executive officer of; Next step: 1) fetch the creator's biography page (e.g., open the 'Simon Cowell' Wikipedia page) and inspect the infobox/lead and 'Career' or 'Positions' sections for current executive roles, 2) open the 'Syco' or 'Syco Entertainment' page and check its 'Leadership'/'History' or infobox to confirm any CEO title. If you mean a different creator of The X Factor, please specify.
hop2_docs: Simon Cowell | Simon Phillip Cowell ( ) (born 7 October 1959) is an English reality television judge and producer, entrepreneur, and philanthropist.  He is most recognised as a judge on the British TV talent competition series "Pop Idol", "The X Factor", and "Britain's Got Talent", and the American TV talent competition shows "American Idol", "The X Factor," and "America's Got Talent".  Cowell is the principal founder and chief executive of the British entertainment company Syco.

Sweet Revenge: The Intimate Life of Simon Cowell | Sweet Revenge: The Intimate Life of Simon Cowell is a 2012 biography of the British music and entertainment figure Simon Cowell by the British investigative journalist and biographer Tom Bower.  Cowell cooperated with the book and a sequel is planned.  The book received a mixed reception from critics.

The X Factor | The X Factor is a television music competition franchise created by Simon Cowell.  It originated in the United Kingdom, where it was devised as a replacement for "Pop Idol" (2001–03).  It is held in various countries.  The programmes are produced by executive producer Simon Cowell and his company SYCOtv.  The "X Factor" of the title refers to the undefinable "something" that makes for star quality.  The prize is usually a recording contract, in addition to the publicity that appearance in the later stages of the show itself generates, not only for the winner but also for other highly ranked contestants.

Nicholas Cowell | Nicholas Andrew Cowell (born 7 March 1961) is a British estate agent, co-founder and director of Estate Office Property Consultants, and head of the Cowell Group.  He is the brother of Simon Cowell and author Tony Cowell and has appeared on the UK television series "The Block" as a judge.
Grease Is the Word | Grease Is the Word was the United Kingdom version of NBC's "".  It aired during 2007, and was produced by Syco TV (the production company of Simon Cowell).

Syco | Syco Entertainment, often known simply as Syco, is a British entertainment company established by British entertainment mogul Simon Cowell.  The company operates a record label, talent agency, film, music, and television production company, as well as a music publishing house.  It operates a global joint venture between Cowell and Sony Music Entertainment focused on the production and marketing of music, television, film and digital content.  It employs a staff of more than 50 in offices in London and Los Angeles, and manages a string of high-profile television and music brands through partnerships with the label Sony Music and the television production company, FremantleMedia.

I Don't Mean to be Rude, but... | I Don't Mean to be Rude, but... is a 2003 autobiography book from popular television personality and music critic Simon Cowell.  The book gives an insight into Simon Cowell's life as well as backstage gossip and tips on how to be successful.
hop2_titles: Sweet Revenge: The Intimate Life of Simon Cowell
I Don't Mean to be Rude, but...
response.summary_2: The creator of The X Factor (Simon Cowell) is chief executive officer of the British entertainment company Syco (Passage [1] — Simon Cowell: “principal founder and chief executive of the British entertainment company Syco”). Passage [3] (“The X Factor”) supplies the metadata that Simon Cowell created the franchise and that programmes are produced by his company SYCOtv; Passage [6] (“Syco”) supplies company metadata that Syco was established by Simon Cowell and describes its operations. Integration: Passage [1] explicitly supports the CEO claim; passages [3] and [6] provide corroborating metadata linking Cowell to SYCO/SYCOtv for downstream use.
